policy_statement: By 2025, publish a Decarbonization Masterplan for systematic decommissioning of natural gas distribution/transmission, specify residual uses likely to remain in 2040, and provide interactive neighborhood planning mechanisms (ES.5-3).
mechanism_description: Publish a decarbonization masterplan that lays out a systematic approach to decommissioning the natural gas distribution and transmission system and provides interactive neighborhood planning to coordinate electrification, reducing reliance on fossil gas.
primary_causal_pathway: Fossil fuel infrastructure phaseout → ↓ fossil gas delivery/combustion + ↓ methane leakage → ↓ GHG emissions → ↓ atmospheric forcing
causal_mechanism_detail: (1) Change in activity/system: Cities plan and execute an orderly retirement of gas distribution/transmission assets and coordinate neighborhood-scale electrification to replace gas end-uses. (2) Mediating physical process: Reduced flow and combustion of fossil methane lowers CO₂ emissions from end-use combustion and reduces fugitive methane emissions from pipelines, meters, and related infrastructure. (3) Climate-relevant outcome: Net reduction in GHG emissions (CO₂ and CH₄), leading to lower cumulative radiative forcing.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reduced greenhouse gas emissions by phasing out fossil gas delivery and use (avoiding CO₂ from combustion and methane leakage from the gas system).
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: Retiring gas networks and coordinating electrification primarily reduces fossil gas use and methane leakage, cutting GHG emissions.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: While it may improve energy efficiency or reduce costs, those are secondary; the central climate function is decarbonization via fossil fuel phaseout (mitigation).  
3) Edge cases applied: If a plan focuses mainly on resilience/safety (e.g., seismic risk) without committing to emissions reductions, it could lean adaptation, but this mechanism explicitly targets decarbonization and gas phaseout. If “renewable gas/hydrogen repurposing” dominates, the mechanism would differ; here it is decommissioning/phaseout.  
4) Confirm applies to ALL instances: Any policy under this mechanism—planning for systematic decommissioning of gas distribution/transmission to enable electrification—has the primary effect of reducing fossil gas-related GHG emissions.  
5) Secondary subtype assignment deferred: Whether the policy’s quantified benefits come more from avoided CO₂ vs avoided CH₄, or from specific end-use electrification measures, requires policy-level evidence and is deferred.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Could be misclassified if the “masterplan” is purely procedural with no credible linkage to actual retirement/fuel-switching, or if it primarily enables continued gaseous fuels via repurposing (hydrogen/biomethane) rather than fossil gas phaseout; otherwise straightforward mitigation.
key_indicators: "By 2025, publish a Decarbonization Masterplan"; "documenting the systematic approach to decommissioning natural gas distribution and transmission"; "Specify... 'residual' in 2040"; "interactive planning mechanisms"; "empower coordination of electrification"; "set localized goals and priorities"
co_benefits: Potential air quality and public health improvements from reduced combustion; potential energy affordability/consumer cost reductions from avoiding gas system fixed costs; safety benefits from reduced gas leak/explosion risk.
instance_edge_case_notes: Although it concerns decommissioning infrastructure, the action required here is plan publication and coordination mechanisms (not the actual retirement or a mandate), so it is classified as a planning-strategy with enabling directness rather than infrastructure-investment or regulatory-mandate.
